summary:
Indonesian independence was proclaimed on 17 August 1945, two days after Japan's surrender ending World War II, by nationalist leaders Sukarno and Mohammad Hatta. The Dutch, who had colonized the Indonesian archipelago for over 300 years as the Dutch East Indies, refused to recognize the proclamation and returned to reclaim their colony after the Japanese occupation, leading to four years of armed conflict and diplomacy known as the Indonesian National Revolution.

The revolution combined guerrilla warfare against Dutch forces with intense international diplomacy. The United States, reluctant to support European colonialism in the postwar era and concerned about driving Indonesian nationalists toward communism, applied significant pressure on the Netherlands to negotiate. UN mediation produced several agreements, but Dutch military operations continued. A major Dutch military offensive in December 1948 triggered international condemnation and American economic pressure that proved decisive.

The Netherlands formally recognized Indonesian sovereignty over most of the archipelago on 27 December 1949, following negotiations at the Hague Round Table Conference. West New Guinea (West Papua) remained under Dutch control until 1962. Indonesia emerged as the world's largest archipelagic state and fourth most populous nation, comprising over 13,000 islands and hundreds of distinct ethnic groups and languages.

The independence struggle shaped Indonesian national identity and established the foundation principles of the Indonesian state, including the Pancasila — five philosophical principles including national unity, humanitarianism, democracy, and social justice — that Sukarno articulated as a unifying framework for the country's extraordinary diversity.
